hydrofoil.title: Hydrofoil Flow-Control Analysis
what: Investigated the effect of passive vortex generators on the performance of a NACA 63-618 tidal turbine hydrofoil.
Conducted CFD simulations in ANSYS Fluent and validated results through wind tunnel testing.
Designed and tested multiple rectangular and triangular vortex generator configurations.
how: Modelled the hydrofoil and vortex generator configurations in SOLIDWORKS.
Performed CFD analysis using ANSYS Fluent with the SST k-ω turbulence model.
Manufactured test models using 3D printing and conducted wind tunnel experiments.
Analysed lift, drag, flow separation, and stall characteristics across different angles of attack.
Identified triangular vortex generators as the best-performing configuration, achieving up to 20% improvement in lift performance.
why: To understand how vortex generators can delay flow separation and improve hydrofoil performance.
To increase lift, reduce drag, and improve the efficiency of tidal turbine blades.
To compare computational predictions with experimental results and assess the effectiveness of different vortex generator designs.

conveyor-system.title: Bottle Twister Mechanism
what: Modelled a 'twister' that reorients bottles on a packaging line
Built the twisted guide by lofting through progressive cross-sections
Ran a motion study of a bottle travelling through the guide
how: Modelled the full geometry in SOLIDWORKS
Assigned 1060 aluminium to the twister and acrylic to the bottle
Set up contact, friction and motion constraints
why: Reorients products automatically, with no manual handling
Showed how friction and guide geometry drive the motion
Validates the design ahead of building a full conveyor

laser-housing.title: Laser Housing Thermal Optimisation
what: Assessed steady-state thermal performance of a finned laser housing
Modelled conjugate heat transfer across water, helium, air and aluminium
Targeted 523 K helium with water and aluminium kept within limits
how: Built a quarter-symmetry CFD model in ANSYS Fluent
Ran a mesh independence study (under 1% variation)
Optimised inputs with ANSYS DesignXplorer (DOE + response surface)
why: Met every temperature target after optimisation
Cut required air velocity and water flow by about 50%
Showed simulation can size the cooling without overdesign

thrust-vector-control.title: Thrust Vector Control for Model Rockets
what: Designed a thrust vector control (TVC) system for model rockets
Replaced fin stabilisation with a twin-servo gimballed nozzle
Five printed parts: motor sleeve, inner and outer gimbals, two linkages
how: Designed for metal AM in Ti-6Al-4V with lattice and spiral features
Optimised part orientation to cut supports and post-processing
Validated each part with SolidWorks static FEA at 12.1 N thrust
why: Kept the assembly light (about 104.5 g) using hollow and lattice structures
Gimbal gives plus-minus 15 degrees of thrust control versus fixed fins
Showed metal AM is viable for small aerospace components

braking-system-reliability.title: Car Braking System Reliability
what: Analysed the reliability of a car disc-braking system
Mapped failure modes across rotor, pads, caliper, shims and fluid
Proposed an improved Al-MMC disc rotor design
how: Applied QFD, HAZOP, FMEA, FTA and block-diagram analysis
Quantified system reliability and load-strength safety margins
Benchmarked materials and added redundancy to the weak parts
why: Raised modelled system reliability from about 0.40 to 0.70
Lifted the stress-strength safety margin from 1.24 to 4.64
Pinpointed the highest-risk failure modes to prioritise

mesh-sensitivity.title: Mesh Sensitivity Study — Conjugate Heat Transfer
what: Ran a five-mesh sensitivity study on a heated copper block in air
Verified the peak temperature became mesh-independent
Extended the analysis to a transient convection case
how: Built structured all-quad meshes with the sweep method
Refined with edge sizing and biasing near the solid–fluid interface
Solved steady conduction and transient convection in Ansys Fluent
why: Confirm results don't depend on mesh density
Keep the element count efficient (under 100k)
Establish a trustworthy baseline for thermal design

sun-tracking-harvester.title: Sun-Tracking Energy Harvester
what: Designed a dual-axis sun-tracking solar harvester for the Mojave Desert
Folding origami panel array on a concrete-free tripod frame
Benchmarked against the Ivanpah solar plant
how: Modelled the assembly and folding mechanism in CAD
Ran steady-state thermal FEA in Ansys to find panel hotspots
Sized motors, wind loads and a cradle-to-grave LCA
why: Cut land use by ~95% versus Ivanpah for equal output
Improve energy capture with dual-axis tracking
Lower lifecycle emissions with a concrete-free foundation
